chosen_title: On Venus a day is longer than its year
label: SPACE
headline: A day longer than a year
image_prompt: the planet Venus rendered hyper-real and ominous, swirling sulfuric-acid clouds half in shadow, deep black space, dramatic rim light
tweets: On Venus, a single day lasts longer than its entire year. No, that's not a typo. 🧵
Venus spins on its axis insanely slowly — one full rotation takes 243 Earth days. But it orbits the Sun in just 225 Earth days. So the planet finishes a lap before it finishes a single spin.
Stranger still: Venus spins backwards. On Venus, the Sun rises in the west and sets in the east — the opposite of every other major planet.
Scientists think an ancient colossal impact, or the Sun's gravitational drag on its thick atmosphere, may have flipped and slowed it over billions of years.
And that atmosphere is brutal: 96% CO2, surface pressure 90x Earth's, hot enough at 465C to melt lead. A runaway greenhouse effect gone nuclear.

Venus is almost Earth's twin in size — but a glimpse of what an out-of-control climate can do to a world.
